4.5.1.2 Material factors

The material properties relevant to EAC in high temperature water include [98]:

‚ÄĘ Alloy composition;
‚ÄĘ Alloy microstructure, especially the extent of grain boundary sensitization;
‚ÄĘ Yield strength;
‚ÄĘ Extent of surface damage/cold work.

 Historically, the extent of Cr depletion at the grain boundary due to growth of
M23C6 carbides has been most associated with SCC susceptibility in austenitic stain-
less steels in BWRs. Grain boundary Cr depletion can be altered by material compo-
sition (e.g., presence of stabilizing elements), stress (i.e., stress-induced martensite
formation), and heat treatment (Fig. 4.26). Grain boundary sensitization can lead to
intergranular crack initiation and propagation, the crack propagation rate depending
on other material attributes (yield strength, etc.) as well as water chemistry and
loading. Cracking is sensitive to small degrees of grain boundary sensitization and,
in particular, the chromium content (Fig. 4.27). This understanding has led to the deÔ¨Ā-
nition of well-proven mitigating actions based on minimizing the extent of grain
boundary sensitization [98].
 A fundamental challenge for the SCWR is that the high peak fuel cladding temper-
ature (up to 800 C) results in slow microstructural evolution due to thermal aging
(diffusion and secondary phase precipitation, particularly involving grain boundaries),
leading to long-term increases in SCC susceptibility [116,120]. Ru and Staehle [35]
predicted that the dynamic thermal range will result in signiÔ¨Ācant aging of materials,
changing grain boundary chemistry and structure, and thus changing SCC susceptibil-
ity. To date most tests have used as-fabricated materials, and the inÔ¨āuence of interme-
tallic phases requiring longer aging times (e.g., chi (c), laves (h) and sigma (s) phases)
has received little attention. These phases are of particular concern for stainless steels
that contain Nb and Ti to intentionally precipitate carbides [120]. With the exception of
the work of Udy et al. [121], who carried out SCC tests at 732 C using static

550 C, 25 MPa SCW with controlled oxygen content. The SSRT specimens were
cold-worked unintentionally as a result of the manufacturing process. Strain rate
(elongation rate) and oxygen content were varied. The results did not show any sig-

IGSCC is the most common mode of cracking in LWRs. Fractographic analysis

 For austenitic stainless steels in SCW, the positive effect of surface cold work
(enhanced corrosion resistance [Section 4.1] and higher yield strength) may be coun-
teracted by increased SCC susceptibility. Additional studies of the effects of cold work
are needed.

used as a threshold parameter for SCC (e.g., ASTM E1681-95 [123]). QuantiÔ¨Ācation
of the effect of K is especially important in understanding the rate-controlling pro-

 Irradiation effects include changes to corrosion potential, applied tensile stress,
grain boundary composition, and yield strength. Those changes caused by radiation
Ô¨āux (i.e., corrosion potential) have an immediate effect on cracking susceptibility,
whereas Ô¨āuence-driven changes (radiation-induced segregation, radiation hardening,
stress relaxation, transmutation effects) have a cumulative effect over an extended
time. The speciÔ¨Āc effects of irradiation on parameters that control crack propagation
in stainless steels must consider [128e132]:

‚ÄĘ The corrosion potential and its change with radiation Ô¨āux;
‚ÄĘ Irradiation-induced changes in grain boundary composition;
‚ÄĘ Irradiation-induced hardening; and
‚ÄĘ For displacement-loaded structures, radiation creep stress relaxation.

 To date, data on IASCC in SCW are not available. Most studies on irradiation ef-
fects on IGSCC in support of SCWR concept development have been performed using
heavy ion irradiation. Proton irradiation of Type 316L stainless steel and Alloy 690

4.5.2 Mechanisms and modeling

SCC results from the synergistic effects of metallurgical, chemical, and mechanical
factors. Various SCC mechanisms and controlling parameters have been proposed,
such as slip-oxidation, oxidation penetration, stress-straining enhanced solid-state
oxidation, hydrogen-related mechanism, and dealloying [135e140]. The general sche-
matic of the oxidationemechanics interactions for SCC in high-temperature water is
shown in Fig. 4.35 [141].

142 Structural Materials for Generation IV Nuclear Reactors

 Depending on combinations of metallurgical/chemical/mechanical conditions, the
transient oxidation can take different kinetic laws, and enhancement of crack tip oxida-
tion can be realized via physical degradation, physical-chemical degradation, or both
[122]. Probably the only systematic study on SCC mechanisms for austenitic stainless
steels in SCW has been published by Arioka et al. [142], who investigated mechanistic
processes by which SCC initiates and propagates. A great similarity between SCC and
creep was observed up to 500 C. The study focused on accumulation of vacancies
(resulting from local stresses) to produce cavities, thus causing initiation and propaga-
tion of SCC. Ru and Staehle [35] pointed out that since the SCW environment will
likely be very oxidizing, it would be surprising if the crack velocity would be very
high, in agreement with the results of Peng et al. [126].
 Assessment of the elemental mechanistic and synergistic effects for a complex pro-
cess such as SCC of austenitic stainless steels in SCW will require systematic work and
in particular mechanistic fracture mechanics-based crack growth rate tests. More work,
especially multiscale modeling based on fundamental mechanisms, is required.

4.6 Summary

It has been long recognized that a reactor operating at supercritical temperatures and
pressures, i.e., the SCWR, is the natural evolution of the WCR technology that gener-
ates most of the electricity produced by nuclear power worldwide. IdentiÔ¨Ācation of ma-
terials for in-core use able to withstand the in-core environment, a combination of high
temperature, high pressure, irradiation, and rapidly changing chemical properties, has
been a major barrier to implementation of this technology.
 The general corrosion of iron- and nickel-based alloys under proposed SCWR in-
core conditions is now fairly well understood, a good mechanistic framework has been
developed, and the remaining gaps in the data required to make reliable extrapolations
to end-of-life for in-core components are being closed. The EAC behavior of candidate
materials is less clear, with sometimes contradictory results being reported by different
groups. Additional work is required to understand and reconcile these differences,
deÔ¨Āne performance requirements, and develop appropriate testing methodologies.
Careful consideration of lessons learned from the extensive LWR operating experi-
ence, coupled with adoption of best practices of LWRs and SCW FFPs regarding water
feedtrain chemistry control will help mitigate EAC in an SCWR. A major gap remains
in understanding the effects of irradiation on EAC.

Enhancing multi-physics modeling in new-generation nuclear reactors
using machine learning: Implementing Gaussian Process Regression for
updating cross sections
Mahdi Aghili Nasr a, Lorenzo Loi a , Stefano Riva a , Ahmad Zolfaghari b, Xiang Wang c ,
Antonio Cammi a,d,*
a Department of Energy, Politecnico di Milano, 20133 Milano, Italy

A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T

Keywords: Due to the complexity of the multi-physics behavior in next-generation Molten Salt Reactors (MSRs), developing
Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) high-fidelity simulation codes is essential. Despite recent advancements in developing these codes, updating
Supervised machine learning cross sections as a function of state variables remains a persistent challenge. In this work, a new method for
Updating the neutron cross sections updating neutron cross sections based on Gaussian Process Regression (GPR), a supervised learning technique isMulti-physics calculation developed to enhance the fidelity of the coupling between neutronic and thermal‚Äďhydraulic solvers. In thisMolten Salt Reactors (MSRs)
 method, GPR is employed in place of the traditional logarithmic correlation (Log) to update cross sections based
 on fuel temperature. The GPR method is integrated into the PoliMi multi-physics code, which is developed on the
 OpenFOAM libraries, and is tested on two cases: the lid-driven cavity benchmark and the 2D axisymmetric MSFR.
 The GPR model is verified using test data generated by Monte Carlo Serpent simulations. The numerical results of
 this study suggest that for systems containing isotopes exhibiting significant absorption resonances (such as Th232
 and U238), the method of updating cross sections has a greater impact on the predicted Keff than the choice of
 neutronic solver (Diffusion or SP3). Additionally, the updating method has a smaller impact on the SP3 solver
 compared to the Diffusion solver. The reactivity injection accident simulation results demonstrate that updating
 cross sections can significantly affect the predicted peak power. This is particularly important in multi-physics
 transient modeling for reactor safety analysis.

 The nTRACER code, developed in 2009, in the decoupled scheme mode,
 uses an online group constant generator for three-dimensional coarse

ture and density, on neutron cross sections. Simulation codes integrating are generated online through cell calculations for all mesh regions
neutronic and thermal‚Äďhydraulic (NTH) phenomena have already been (Akbari et al., 2020). The NRCC code, created in 2020, utilizes average
developed for commercial Light Water Reactors (LWR). For example, the current nodal expansion for neutronic calculations, a two-phase single
NESTLE code, developed in 1994, employs the nodal expansion method heated channel for thermal‚Äďhydraulic calculations, and a method for
for eigenvalue calculations and the Homogeneous Equilibrium Mixture cross section interpolation from neutronic library files (Nasr and Zan¬≠

series expansion in terms of coolant density, coolant temperature, the LWR cores, such as PARCS (Downar et al., 2010) and DONJON (Varin
square root of fuel temperature, and dissolved poison concentration to et al., 2005), approximately use similar methods for NTH calculations
update the macroscopic cross sections (Turinsky and Al-chalabi, 1994).

 To train the machine learning model, a series of Serpent simulations
 are performed to collect HMGXS data at different temperatures. Spe¬≠
 cifically, 15 simulations are carried out with homogeneous fuel tem¬≠
 peratures ranging uniformly from 800 K to 2400 K. The dataset
 generated from these simulations is randomly split into 80 % for training
 and 20 % for testing (model verification). Since the model is blind to the
 testing data, these points are used to quantify the prediction error where
 the HMGXS are known.
 Figs. 2, 3, and 4, respectively, illustrate the temperature-dependent
 behavior of the diffusion coefficient (D), fission cross section (ő£f), and
 absorption cross section (ő£a) obtained from Serpent simulations,
 comparing the Log and GPR predictions across the three most important
 energy groups. In these figures, the training data are marked with black
 dots, while the test data are indicated by purple squares.
 From a qualitative perspective, the strength of the GPR method lies
 in its ability to follow the training data, though at a high computational
 cost. On the other hand, the Log method estimates only the average
 trend in temperature with a significantly lower computational demand.
 The test set, comprising 3 out of the 15 total points for each group in
 each HMGXS, is used for verification purposes. Both the GPR and Log
 method predictions are compared against the Serpent reference values.

 3.2. 2D axisymmetric MSFR

 The 2D axisymmetric MSFR is a conceptual design in frame of the
 EVOL project (Brovchenko et al., 2013). A slice of geometry is displayed
 in Fig. 6, along with details about the positions of the heat exchanger,
 pump, and core cavity sections. The heat exchanger is considered as a
 heat sink using the same equation as Eq. (17). The pump is modeled by
 applying a momentum source in the vertical direction. Additionally, the
 standard k ‚ąíőĶ model is used for turbulence modeling of the MSFR. The
 reference design is intended for a nominal thermal power of 3 GWth,
 with the fuel salt temperature increasing by őĒT = 100K during the
 primary loop. Table 2 summarizes the key parameters of the 2D
 axisymmetric MSFR.

Nuclear Engineering and Design 435 (2025) 113920

 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect

Gen-IV pool reactors: Validation of sloshing CFD modelling and behavior
under large seismic events‚ėÜ
Vincent Moreau * , Manuela Profir
CRS4 Pula, Sardinia, Italy

A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T

Keywords: This paper addresses one aspect of the safety of compact lead-cooled pool fast reactors. Under a seismic event,
ALFRED there is concern that the surface sloshing could compromise the integrity of some structures or components. We
PASCAL investigate this issue by means of computational modelling and simulation using the STAR-CCM + commercial
FALCON software.CFD In the first part, we simulate a partially filled vertical cylinder under harmonic forcing and quantitativelySloshing
VoF validate the modelling by comparison with experimental data of the free-surface height at different locations.
 Specifically, we capture with a very high precision a behavioral transition of the amplitude-frequency map near
 resonance.
 In the second part, the modelling is applied to a fast reactor mock-up in water. It is validated again by
 comparison with experimental data under harmonic forcing. The numerical mock-up is then subjected to four
 seismic events of increasing intensity. The free-surface height and the forces applied to the mock-up vessel are
 monitored.
 It is found that the forces applied to the vessel are strongly dominated by the highest frequency of the seismic
 acceleration. By applying a high filter cut at 1 Hz, the peak force is reduced by one order of magnitude but
 remains strongly related to the damped acceleration signal. Even then, sloshing forcing effects are observed but
 remain marginal.

Safety assessment of MSR concept using INPRO methodology T
Muhammad Hassan Mohsina, Kamran Qureshib,*, Talha Ashfaqa

a Department of Nuclear Engineering, Pakistan Institute of Engg & App Sciences (PIEAS), Islamabad, Pakistan
b Department of Mechanical Engineering, Pakistan Institute of Engg & App Sciences (PIEAS), Islamabad, Pakistan

A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T

Keywords: The share of energy from nuclear source is constantly increasing globally. With the advancement in every walk
Molten salt reactor (MSR) of life, be it social; economic or technological; there is one common factor which is energy. To meet these energy
INPRO methodology needs, nuclear power has to contribute its share in a sustainable way. In order to assess the sustainability of any
Safety assessment nuclear energy system (NES), IAEA's International Project on Innovative Nuclear Power Reactor and Fuel Cycles
 (INPRO) has developed a methodology known INPRO methodology. It covers seven diÔ¨Äerent areas including
 economics, infrastructure, safety, waste management, environment, physical protection and proliferation re-
 sistance. In the present paper, safety of molten salt reactor (MSR) concept has been assessed using INPRO
 methodology. MSR is one of the six designs being pursued by GEN IV Forum. All the basic principles, user
 requirements, criteria in the area of safety of INPRO methodology have been assessed based on the data available
 in open literature. It is observed that the MSR technology ticks most of the boxes in terms of safety. However,
 some gaps have also been identiÔ¨Āed which need further R&D for this concept.

1. Introduction nuclear reactors successfully around the globe to meet high energy
 demands, public sentiment towards nuclear has to be changed. In this
 With the increase in energy demand, the share of energy from nu- regard, GEN IV International Forum (GIF) has identiÔ¨Āed eight diÔ¨Äerent
clear sources is increasing day by day. Today we see applications of technology goals in the areas of sustainability; safety; economics; and
nuclear energy in various Ô¨Āelds such as agriculture, medicine, power physical protection and proliferation resistance (Levy and
sector, desalination, food irradiation and nuclear space reactors etc. In TodreasRalph Bennett, 2001). These goals have been deÔ¨Āned keeping in
the power sector, conventional sources of energy like oil and gas are view the social, economic and environmental requirements of the 21st
neither sustainable nor renewable, while the nuclear energy has the century. GIF has selected six diÔ¨Äerent reactor concepts which would be
potential to serve as both. Nuclear energy provides reliable and base- safer, cleaner, cost-eÔ¨Äective, and more resistant against proliferation
load electricity. Energy from nuclear source is available for long hours and terrorist attacks compared to existing reactors. These reactors are
and continuously. It is a green energy, causing no danger to our en- designed with lower Core Damage Frequency (CDF) and lower Large
vironment in terms of emission of the greenhouse gases (GHGs). In Early Release Frequency (LERF). These reactors are judged by de-
comparison to other energy sources, fuel consumption is very low in terministic and probabilistic approaches at every step of their devel-
nuclear energy systems; hence, it can provide immense power in a re- opment, ultimately ensuing in a safe and sustainable reactor.
latively small volume compared to other energy sources. At present, Since nuclear power is considered to be a sustainable option and
12% of the world's electricity is generated by nuclear and this much allows the nations to achieve the goals of sustainable development,
electricity is being produced from 448 nuclear reactors globally therefore it is important to check the sustainability aspect of any nu-
(International Atomic Energy Agency, 2017). clear energy system (NES) including innovative nuclear energy system
 With the word nuclear comes the negative sentiment of general (INS) used for generating electricity. Sustainable development is de-
public as nuclear industry has suÔ¨Äered its share of unfortunate events in Ô¨Āned as: Development that meets the needs of the present without compro-
the past. Accidents like Three Mile Island (TMI),Chernobyl and mising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs
Fukushima raise question against the safety of nuclear reactors. As (International Atomic Energy Agency, 2008). Sustainable development
discussed, to fulÔ¨Āll the higher energy demand in various walks of life is linked with social, environment, and economic dimension. In year
requires, the use of nuclear is essential. However, in order to deploy 2000, the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) launched the

Stability and bifurcation analysis of Generation IV reactors via point reactor
models with temperature reactivity feedback
Doron Sivan a,b,‚ąó, Ehud Meron a,c, Shai Kinast d

a Physics Department, Ben-Gurion University of the Negev, Beer Sheva 8410501, Israel
b Israel Atomic Energy Commission, Tel-Aviv 61070, Israel
c The Swiss Institute for Dryland Environmental and Energy Research, BIDR, Ben-Gurion University of the Negev, Midreshet Ben-Gurion, 8499000, Israel
d Nuclear Research Center ‚Äď Negev, P.O. Box 9001, 84190 Beer-Sheva, Israel

A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T

Keywords: While the stability of present-day reactors is well studied, stability analysis of Generation IV reactors is in
Nuclear reactor stability analysis its very beginning. In this paper, the stability of point nuclear reactor models with temperature reactivity
Bifurcation analysis feedbacks is analyzed. The examined models are referred to as G-L reactor models, which are point reactor
Point reactor model models with ūĚźļdelayed neutron-emission groups, ūĚźŅtemperature lumps (regions) and a linear reactivity
Temperature reactivity feedback
 feedback for each temperature. G-L reactor models are applicable for modeling four out of the six Generation IV
Generation IV reactors
 reactor types (Gas Fast Reactor, Lead Fast Reactor, Sodium Fast Reactor and Very High Temperature Reactor).Microreactor
 New analytical stability and bifurcation results of general G-L reactor models and of the G1-L2 reactor
 model are derived. These analytical results add a different perspective to the previous studies of Generation
 IV reactors, most of which are based only on numerical analysis. In particular, the codimension-1 bifurcations
 of general G-L reactor models are characterized, and a classification of the linear stability region in the G1-L2
 model is derived.
 Then, stability and bifurcation analysis of a G1-L2 model of a Generation IV HTR-type microreactor is
 performed, using both analytical methods and the numerical continuation method. The nominal design point
 of this microreactor is shown to be globally asymptotically stable, while in other regions of the parameter
 space, a bistability of the operating steady state and a limit cycle, or of two limit cycles, is demonstrated.

1. Introduction reactor stability of Generation IV reactors is currently in its beginning.
 As few Generation IV prototype reactors are (or were) in operation,
 Generation IV nuclear reactors are new advanced reactor designs the focus of the stability studies is theoretical. Stability of six reference
that represent a considerable departure from Generation II and III Generation IV reactor designs, representing the six different Generation
nuclear reactors, with major leaps in sustainability, safety, economics, IV reactor types, was studied in Cervi et al. (2018). Other studies
proliferation resistance and physical protection (GIF, 2018). Six Gener- concentrated on a single Generation IV type, e.g. LFR (Bortot et al.,
ation IV reactor types are being considered: Gas-cooled Fast Reactor 2013; Shahzad et al., 2018), MSR (Guerrieri et al., 2013; Singh et al.,
(GFR), Lead-cooled Fast Reactor (LFR), Molten Salt Reactor (MSR), 2018), SCWR (Yi et al., 2004; Zhao et al., 2008) and SFR (Kim et al.,
Super-Critical Water-cooled Reactor (SCWR), Sodium-cooled Fast Re- 2016).
actor (SFR), and Very High Temperature Reactor (VHTR). The types

with the most research and development progress to date are SFR and

VHTR (GIF, 2018).

the past concentrated on two types of instabilities observed in Gen-
 IV reactor types, is studied via both analytical and numerical methods.
eration II reactors: Boiling Water Reactor (BWR) oscillations (due to

 can provide more insight into the stability of realistic Generation IVXenon oscillations (due to coupling between neutronics and reactor
burnup) (Bell and Glasstone, 1970). In contrast, the study of nuclear reactors. In the 1950s through the 1970s, many analytical studies of

4. G-L reactor models: linear stability analysis

 In this section, two results about general G-L reactor models are
derived. First, it is shown that under plausible assumptions, the uncou-
pled thermal-hydraulics part of the G-L reactor model is linearly stable.
Second, the codimension-1 bifurcations of the operating steady state, in
which a loss of the linear stability of this state occurs, are characterized.

documents.text:
GEN IV International Forum

 ANNUAL
 REPORT
 2023

 www.gen-4.org More about the Generation IV International Forum:
 Further details about GIF activities can be found on: https://www.gen-4.org
 1

Foreword
 from the Chair

 The Generation IV International Forum (GIF) 2023 Annual Report highlights the
 achievements and progress of research and development collaborations for Generation IV
 (Gen IV) reactor systems.

 The year 2023 has been productive for the GIF, with many accomplishments in the form of
 publications, webinars and outreach, in addition to the technical progress made on the six
 GIF systems. I particularly enjoyed our in-person Policy and Experts Group meetings that
 provided much-needed interactions and time for side conversations. Thank you to France
 for hosting the April meeting in Lyon.

 The development of a new framework agreement to continue the work of the GIF among
 mutually willing parties began in 2023 and will come into effect from February 2025. It is
important that the GIF continues to provide global leadership and support through the new framework to
ensure that safe, secure and sustainable nuclear energy is available for the future.

The Advanced Manufacturing and Materials Engineering (AMME) Task Force was approved to transition to
a working group (AMME-WG) in October 2023, with a leadership role in the manufacturing and materials
community. AMME-WG objectives are to promote the use of advanced manufacturing and materials
engineering technology, thus reducing time to deployment for advanced reactor systems. Specifically,
the AMME-WG aims to promote international collaboration on the qualification of advanced materials and
manufacturing processes for use in Gen IV reactors. We are excited to learn about their future accomplishments
in this important research area.

We continue to look for opportunities to increase our engagement with industry and vendors to accelerate
the demonstration of Gen IV systems. Opportunities for direct industry contributions or data sharing are
being pursued in GIF technical projects. With the increased interest in advanced nuclear energy systems and
crosscutting applications to benefit from low-carbon electricity and heat to meet global carbon reduction
goals, the GIF Task Force on Non-Electric Applications of Nuclear Heat (NEANH) plans to organize a second
NEANH workshop in 2024 with broad industry participation. The GIF also continues to work with the GIF
Senior Industry Advisory Panel to focus on the transition of research and development to demonstration and
commercial deployment.

The second Pitch your Gen IV Research competition was conducted to increase junior researchers‚Äô
engagement within the Gen IV community and stimulate their interest in advanced reactor systems. Forty-
seven abstracts were submitted by participants from 11 different countries. Eleven selected participants
produced short, informative videos, on which over 400 members of the public voted. The first, second and
third place winners were invited to present a GIF webinar in 2024.

As we move forward, the GIF will continue to strengthen Gen IV systems to support areas such as non-electric
applications, climate change, technical readiness, regulatory harmonization and economic improvement in
support of the commercial deployment of advanced systems. I look forward to another successful year!

With best wishes,

 Alice Caponiti
 GIF Chair

 GIF 2023 ANNUAL REPORT 1

A tribute

 With deep sorrow, the Nuclear Power Plant Research Institute of Slovakia (VUJE,
 a.s.) conveyed the passing of Dr. Branislav Hatala, the Director of the Nuclear Safety,
 Research, and Development Division. Dr. Hatala was a longstanding member of the
 Generation IV International Forum (GIF), serving as the chair of the Gas-cooled Fast
 Reactor System (GFR) Steering Committee and making significant contributions to
 the development of the ALLEGRO GFR demonstrator.

 Dr. Hatala joined VUJE, a.s., in 1995 and contributed to safety assessment of
 VVER‚ÄĎ440 units operated in Slovakia. Thermo-hydraulic analyses and the theory of
 thermomechanical behavior of nuclear fuel were truly his passion. Notably, his early
 work earned him the Award of the Minister of Education of the Slovak Republic for
 Science and Technology, recognizing his significant achievements in safety analysis
 methodology. Under his leadership, his team was honored as the ‚ÄúScientific and
 Technical Team of the Year‚ÄĚ in 2017 for their groundbreaking work on the ALLEGRO
 reactor. Branislav was a highly valued contributor to GIF and the nuclear community
 for almost 29 years. He will truly be missed and always remembered.

Foreword
 from the Chair

 It is a privilege to compose this foreword for the Generation IV International Forum (GIF) 2021
 Annual Report, which outlines last year‚Äôs progress in Generation-IV (Gen-IV) reactor systems‚Äô
 collaboration and developments. GIF completed its second decade as the sole international
 organization dedicated to collaborative research and development (R&D) on Gen-IV systems.
 Since 2001, GIF has been promoting international R&D collaboration for six types of Gen-IV reactor
 systems using sodium, lead, gas, molten salt and supercritical water coolants. These systems
 follow the common development goals established by GIF: safety and economics, together with
 sustainability

GEN IV International Forum

 ANNUAL
 REPORT
 2022

 www.gen-4.org More about the Generation IV International Forum:
 Further details about GIF activities can be found on: https://www.gen-4.org
 1

Foreword
 from the Chair

 The Generation IV International Forum (GIF) 2022 Annual Report highlights the
 achievements and progress in the Generation IV (Gen IV) reactor systems research and
 development.

 This year I was grateful to preside over the first in-person Policy Group meeting for GIF
 members since the COVID-19 pandemic began and welcomed representatives from ten
 GIF member countries in Sydney, Australia. While there was a virtual component of this
 meeting, the importance of meeting in person was significant, providing the time needed
 for discussions to move GIF initiatives forward and the opportunity for side conversations.
 The GIF has had a productive year, with many accomplishments in the form of publications,
 webinars and outreach, in addition to the technical progress made on the six GIF systems.
 Under the leadership of the GIF Policy Director, Fiona Rayment, the GIF refreshed its
Policy Statement on Governance and created a new Policy Statement on Industry Engagement.

A significant achievement in 2022 was hosting the first GIF Industry Forum that was co-located with the
Generation IV and Small Reactors fourth conference (G4SR-4) in Toronto, Canada. The GIF Industry
Forum was co-organized with the Canadian Nuclear Society with the objective for GIF members to engage
with industry and vendors and the small modular reactor (SMR) community. Combining the two events
successfully provided opportunities for the private and public sectors to discuss and collaborate to accelerate
demonstration of Gen IV systems. The technical sessions on advanced nuclear energy systems and workshops
on cross-cutting applications were well attended by nuclear developers and industry sectors seeking to
benefit from low-carbon electricity and heat to meet global carbon reduction goals. My appreciation goes
to Bob Hill, Sylvestre Pivet, Philippe Guiberteau, Gina Abdelsalam and John Kelly for coordinating this first
Industry Forum.

Many thanks are extended to Hideki Kamide for developing and executing one of the forum highlights ‚Äď
Innovation Night. This was a special social event that showcased early career researchers and their experience
with Gen IV/SMR systems. Winners shared their career paths, discussed the challenges they faced in moving
a concept forward and highlighted the rewards of working in the advanced nuclear field. Feedback received
from industry has been very positive, with requests to continue the GIF Industry Forum every two years.

Looking forward, the GIF will continue to provide global leadership and support through the GIF Framework
to ensure that safe, secure and sustainable nuclear energy is available for the future. Over the next decade,
the GIF will continue to strengthen Gen IV systems to support areas such as non-electric applications, climate
change, technical readiness, regulatory harmonization and economic improvement in support of commercial
deployment of advanced systems. In addition, the GIF plans to enhance industry engagement through the
GIF Senior Industry Advisory Panel to focus on the transition of research and development to demonstration
and commercial deployment.

 Alice Caponiti
 GIF Chair

GEN IV International Forum

 ANNUAL
 REPORT
 2024

 www.gen-4.org More about the Generation IV International Forum:
 Further details about GIF activities can be found on: https://www.gen-4.org
 1

Foreword
 from the Chair

The year 2024 has been productive for GIF, with significant technical progress made on the six GIF systems,
as well as many accomplishments in the form of publications, webinars and outreach. GIF was pleased to
roll out its refreshed public-facing website to better showcase the value GIF offers to the advanced nuclear
technology community, an accomplishment which owes much appreciation to the hard work of the Nuclear
Energy Agency (NEA) as the GIF Technical Secretariat. We are also grateful to Euratom for hosting the May
meeting in Brussels and the United Kingdom for hosting the October meeting in London. These meetings
fostered collaboration and allowed us to celebrate our work.

To extend the impact of Gen IV technology, GIF collaborations continued to explore the potential for
diversified energy products ranging from electricity to numerous heat applications. The Non-Electric
Applications of Nuclear Heat Task Force was approved to transition to a working group, renamed Non-Electric
and Cogeneration Applications (NECA), in October 2024. The overall objective of the NECA Working Group is
to advance knowledge and access to energy system design, analysis and optimization tools to allow decision
makers to find optimal energy system solutions.

The development of a new framework agreement to continue the work of GIF among mutually willing parties
began in 2023 and went into effect on 1 March 2025. This new framework agreement allows GIF to continue
to provide global leadership and support to ensure that safe, secure, resilient and sustainable nuclear energy
is available for the future.

The 2025-2027 leadership team aims to keep the organization at the forefront of technological innovation
by pursuing international collaborations to accelerate the deployment of Gen IV systems. The project for the
next three years is based on two main pillars: 1) the capitalization of acquired expertise; and 2) expansion into
new actors, applications and markets beyond electricity production.

An essential goal is to foster collaboration with start-ups specializing in advanced reactors and integrate new
participants. To achieve this, we will promote an integrated vision of reactors and fuel cycles to maximize
the efficiency of technologies and provide decarbonization solutions for various sectors, such as heat,
hydrogen and desalination. We will also strengthen our ties with international organizations and industry,
while enhancing GIF‚Äôs communication to increase its visibility and attract new talent.

With best wishes,

St√©phane Sarrade Alice Caponiti
2025-2027 2022-2024
GIF Policy Group Chair GIF Policy Group Chair

 ALLEGRO demonstration project overview

 The ALLEGRO project, coordinated by the V4G4
 Centre of Excellence (EU), continues to progress as
 the primary demonstrator for GFR technology. The
 reactor, designed for a nominal power of 75 MWt,
 features two distinct core configurations. The initial
 core uses proven mixed oxide (MOX) and enriched
 uranium oxide (UO2) fuel with stainless steel clad-
 ding to support the testing of experimental fuel
 assemblies with advanced ceramic fuel. Subsequent
 phases will implement an all-ceramic core to achieve
 higher operating temperatures, power densities and
 fast neutron fluences.

 Key features of the ALLEGRO reactor include:
 ‚ÄĘ Thermal power: A nominal output of 75 MW, allow-
 ing for controlled experimental conditions and
 scalable insights for future commercial reactors.
 ‚ÄĘ Core configurations: The initial core employs MOX
 and UO2 fuels with stainless steel cladding, transi-
 tioning to advanced ceramic cores in later phases.
 ‚ÄĘ Safety systems: Passive decay heat removal mech-
 anisms form a cornerstone of its design, reducing Source: UJZ (2025).
 reliance on active safety systems during off-normal
 events.

High-Fidelity CFD Simulation of Mixed-Convection and

 Forced-Convection in a Pebble Bed Test Reactor Core

Haomin Yuan,‚ąó,a Tri Nguyen,b David Reger,b Elia Merzari,a,b John Acierno,b
Michael Seneca,b Dezhi Dai,a Dillon Shaver,a Ka-Yen Yau,c Giacomo Busco,c
 Nate Salpeter,c R. Brian Jackson,c and Etienne Demarlyc

 aNuclear Science and Engineering Division, Argonne National Laboratory
 9700 South Cass Ave, Bldg 208, Argonne, IL, 60439

 bDepartment of Nuclear Engineering, Pennsylvania State University
 206 Hallowell Building, University Park, PA 16802-4400

 cKairos Power LLC
 707 W Tower Ave Ste A, Alameda, CA 94501
 ‚ąóEmail: hyuan@anl.gov

 Number of pages: 40

 Number of tables: 4

 Number of figures: 27

Abstract

 The Hermes low-power (35 MWth) reactor will be built and operated by Kairos Power LLC

to demonstrate their Fluoride cooled high-temperature reactor (KP-FHR) technology. In the

KP-FHR, the reactor core is composed of randomly packed pebbles with TRISO fuel particles

inside with FLiBe flows upward through the core acting as a coolant. Previous numerical and

experiment studies are either limited to a small-size bed or lack of detailed measurement for heat

transfer. To address the lack of high-fidelity heat transfer data in a real-size FHR core, in this

study, we simulated a pebble bed core with 34374 pebbles randomly packed, similar to the Hermes

reactor‚Äôs size. The core radius is 14 times of the pebble-diameter, while the core height is 45

times. In this work, we are particularly interested in mixed convection regime, where buoyancy

is important. Therefore, we performed several Large-Eddy Simulations (LES) at different Re

numbers (160 ‚ąí1000) with gravitational force included. The Spectral Element CFD code NekRS

with GPU acceleration was used for this study. The low-Mach number approximation has been

applied to address property changes in the FLiBe and account for buoyancy. A pure hexahedral

mesh with 60 million elements was generated by the Voronoi-cell method. At the polynomial

order of 5, total Degrees of Freedoms (DoF) is 7.5 billion. The developed case in this work is the

first of its kind in terms of the size and complexity. The local numerical data across the domain

is obtained and compared with empirical correlations. After examining the data, we found the

following conclusions. For pressure drop, Reger‚Äôs correlation predicts less than 5% error. On the

other hand, for heat transfer, Wakao‚Äôs correlation outperforms the others. Based on our findings,

we recommend the use of Wakao‚Äôs correlation for Nusselt number calculation and for pressure drops

KTA among the available experimental correlations. Reger‚Äôs DNS-driven correlation for pressure

drops should be considered given its best agreement with our calculations.

Keywords ‚ÄĒ Spectral Element Method, NekRS, Pebble Bed Test Reactor, Mixed Convection

 2

I. INTRODUCTION

 Kairos Power, LLC (KP), is developing its Fluoride salt-cooled High-temperature Reactor

(KP-FHR). It is a novel advanced reactor technology that leverages TRISO fuel in pebble form

combined with a low-pressure fluoride salt coolant [1]. KP-FHR is a U.S.-developed advanced

reactor technology based on rapid progress in the last decade. The pre-conceptual FHR designs

developed over the past decade adopt different fuel geometries, power cycles, and power levels.

Most recently, the University of California at Berkeley developed the Mark 1 pebble-bed FHR

(Mk1 PB-FHR) [2], incorporating lessons learned from previous pre-conceptual designs. The fun-

damental concept of an FHR is the novel combination of TRISO particle fuel and chemically-inert,

neutronically-transparent fluoride salt coolant FLiBe (Li-7 enriched Li2BeF4). This combination,

 4

along with other design features such as a low-pressure boundary, high thermal efficiency, and ro-

bust fully-passive safety systems, enables a safe, reliable, and low construction-cost reactor system

which can directly compete with natural gas-fired power plants.

 Kairos Power plans to build and operate the Hermes low-power demonstration reactor at

Oak Ridge to demonstrate its capability to deliver clean, safe, and affordable nuclear heat. The

Hermes reactor will be designed to achieve a thermal power level of 35 MWth, compared to 320

MWth for Kairos Power‚Äôs future commercial reactors. The construction and operation of Hermes

is a key step to commercializing the KP-FHR. The Kairos Power Hermes reactor (KPH) will ad-

vance the iterative development process from prototype to commercial scale by verifying complete

nuclear systems, testing the supply chain, promoting KP manufacturing capabilities for critical

components, and progressing licensing confidence for the KP-FHR.

 Previous studies on flow and heat transfer in random pebble beds have been conducted

either experimentally or numerically. Various researchers have performed experiments [3][4][5][6]

to investigate flow and heat transfer in random pebble beds. However, in experiments focused

on heat transfer, due to limitations in measurement techniques, only global measurements are

typically available. Obtaining high-fidelity local data remains extremely difficult. On the other

hand, explicit numerical simulations of pebble beds have only become feasible in the last two

decades. Historically, high-fidelity numerical studies, such as those using LES and DNS, were

limited to structured beds where pebbles are arranged in an ordered pattern [7][8][9][10][11]. This

assumption, however, deviates significantly from reality. With advancements in computational

power and meshing techniques [12], high-fidelity numerical simulations of random pebble beds

have become possible. During the development of Nek5000 and NekRS, high-fidelity simulations

of random pebble beds were successfully attempted. This work began with a small bed of 146

pebbles [13] and eventually scaled up to 44,257 pebbles [14]. The largest pebble bed simulated

to date contains 350,000 pebbles [15], though in this case, the pebbles were assumed not to be in

contact, which reduced the problem‚Äôs complexity. Based on previous research, both experimental

and numerical, various correlations for pressure drop [16] [17] [18] [19] and heat transfer [20] [21] [22]

[23] [24] [25] have been proposed. Generally, these correlations perform within an error margin of ¬Ī

20%, which still introduces significant uncertainty. This necessitates the use of large safety margins

in construction, thereby increasing costs. However, these correlations have never been validated

 5

against high-fidelity local data in large-scale pebble beds with molten salt flow. Therefore, there

remains a gap in the availability of high-fidelity numerical data for realistic, large-scale random

pebble bed cores.

 As previously discussed, our objective is to study the KPH core in this work. However, the

exact configuration of the KPH core was not available. To address this, we utilized a cylindrical core

model and conducted a high-fidelity full-scale CFD simulation using the Large-Eddy Simulation

(LES) capability in NekRS [14] [25]. With its ability to leverage exa-scale computing [26], NekRS

achieves superior computational speed through GPU acceleration [27]. Fig. (1) illustrates a scaling

test of NekRS on a GPU using a 17x17 rod bundle case. As shown, the computational time per

step (tstep [sec]) continues to decrease even at strong scaling. At an optimal point, the tstep falls

below 0.1 seconds, representing a significant acceleration compared to Nek5000 and other codes

when solving realistic problems. While the aforementioned advantages make NekRS an excellent

tool for high-fidelity simulations, there are also some drawbacks. Like most high-fidelity methods,

this approach requires large computational resources to ensure sufficient resolution. Additionally,

NekRS, similar to its predecessor Nek5000, mandates the use of pure hexahedral meshes for its

Spectral Element Method (SEM) implementation. This requirement presents a significant challenge

for complex geometries, such as random pebble beds. Fortunately, with the development of the

tet-to-hex method [28], many of the meshing challenges can be resolved. However, simulating a

random pebble bed of KPH size still requires a specialized technique, which will be discussed in a

later section. In this work, we developed a case to simulate a full-scale KPH-size pebble bed core

without any periodicity or symmetry, providing the most detailed simulation to date and delivering

unprecedented insight into the system.

 This paper is structured as follows: Firstly, we provide a detailed description of the problem.

Secondly, we present the numerical method employed in the simulation. Next, we discuss all

empirical correlations compared in the study. Before delving into the results discussion, we validate

the method against experimental data, excluding heat transfer considerations. Following this, we

verify it using numerical data from other sources. In the results discussion section, we initially

focus on the global Nu number, and then we further analyze the domain by dividing it into

annular sub-domains for localized data examination. Throughout, we compare our findings with

the mentioned empirical correlations. Lastly, we conclude this work by summarizing our key

 6

Fig. 1. Strong scaling of NekRS on GPU [27]

findings in a dedicated section.

II. PROBLEM DESCRIPTION

 In this work, a KPH-size pebble bed with 34374 randomly packed pebbles is modeled, with

an averaged porosity of 0.384. The pebbles have a uniform diameter of 4 cm. The core radius is

14 times of the pebble-diameter (56 cm), while the height is 45 times (180 cm). In this section,

a serial of fluid-only wall-resolved LES simulations of the modeled KPH-size core have been per-

formed, at various Re numbers and powers. Since simulations are fluid-only, powers are translated

into uniform heat flux values on pebble surfaces. The Prandtl number at inlet condition is 24,

however, it drops below 10 when heated up in the domain. The approach to use uniform heat

flux does affect local temperature distribution, however it does a negligible impact on the Nusselt

number calculation. This observation has been confirmed by our previous publications [29]. These

setpoints where selected to ensure buoyancy were present in the simulation. The low-Mach number

approximation [30], has been applied to consider changing density and the buoyancy effect. To

generate the mesh, the location of each pebble was generated using a Discrete Element Method

(DEM) calculation. A Voronoi cell method [12] was then used to generate a pure hexahedral

mesh of 60 million elements, corresponding to 7.5 billion grid points with a 5th order polynomial

 7

approximation, as shown in Fig. (2). Fig. (3) shows a zoomed in view of the the mesh near side

wall, with red arrows representing the added heat flux while blue arrows indicating cooling. Due

to the size of this case, a mesh sensitivity study was not performed based on this mesh. But it

was performed in our previous work [29] on a smaller bed, showing a sufficient mesh resolution is

reached.

 The simulation has been performed using NekRS on the Summit supercomputer across 3600

GPUs with the maximum time step of 5 √ó 10‚ąí5 s. A total of three cases were simulated in this

work, corresponding to inlet Reynolds numbers of 160, 500, and 1000, to study flow scenarios

ranging from strong to moderate buoyancy. These conditions are also provided by Kairos Power to

simulate real operating scenarios. The detailed simulation conditions are documented in Table I,

while Table II shows the correlations used to evaluate FliBe properties. The instantaneous field of

velocity magnitude and temperature in the non-dimensional unit for Case 1 are shown in Fig. (4).

The final data shown in this paper was collected during about 1.5 flow-through-times for each case.

 The novelty of this work can be summarized as follows and is also presented in Table III. This

study involves the largest pebble bed simulated using explicit CFD under near-realistic conditions.

To accomplish this, we developed a new meshing algorithm capable of managing the large number

of pebbles and their contacts. Considerable effort was also invested in optimizing NekRS for peak

performance in these scenarios. For the first time, detailed numerical data has been obtained for

a pebble bed of this size. The data was used to validate existing correlations, and we offer rec-

ommendations based on the findings. This case study specifically targets the Hermes test reactor,

 8

currently under construction. The data generated provides valuable insights for the operation of

Hermes, benefiting both Kairos Power and the broader nuclear community.

 TABLE III
 Novelty table

 3 Targeted to the Hermes test reactor

III. NUMERICAL METHOD

 NekRS [14], the GPU variant of Nek5000, was developed to leverage the computational

power of GPUs. The solver is based on 2nd or 3rd-order time-splitting of the incompressible

Navier‚ÄďStokes equations with an exponentially convergent spectral element-based discretization

in space [14]. The kernels in NekRS are based on an early fork of libParanumal [31] and were

tailored and expanded to meet the specific requirements of large-scale turbulent flow applications

in complex domains. NekRS provides access to the standard Nek5000 interface and features (e.g.,

conjugate heat transfer), which allows users to leverage existing application-specific source code and

data files on GPU-based platforms. The GPU kernels are written in the portable Open Concurrent

 10

Compute Abstraction (OCCA) library [32] to abstract between different parallel languages such as

OpenCL, CUDA, and HIP. OCCA allows developers to implement parallel kernel code in a slightly

decorated C++ language, OKL (Occa Kernal Language). At runtime, the user can specify which

parallel programming model to target, after which OCCA translates the OKL source code into the

desired target language and Just-In-Time (JIT) compiles kernels for the user‚Äôs target hardware

architecture. In recent applications of NekRS, we have successfully simulated a full pebble bed

core [15] and SMR core [33, 34].

 NekRS solves the Navier-Stokes equations using the Spectral Element Method (SEM) [35][14].

The SEM is a subclass of Galerkin methods or weighted residual methods. It is non-dissipative and

delivers low numerical dispersion. The foundational idea is to minimize the error of the numerical

computation in the energy norm over a chosen space of piecewise polynomials. Overall, the method

presents key similarities and a common theoretical framework with the finite element method

(FEM), but it typically achieves the same accuracy with fewer DOFs as the FEM is constrained

to low polynomial orders due to computational cost [36]. In NekRS, the incompressible Navier-

Stokes equations are solved on Gauss-Lobatto-Legendre quadrature points within an element using

Lagrange polynomial basis functions [37].

 For LES, the SubGrid-Scale (SGS) model typically employed with the SEM relies on forms of

filtering [38]: The energy content of the last few wave numbers is filtered to mimic the effect of SGS

dissipation [39]. This could be considered related to other SGS models, such as deconvolution LES,

hyper-viscosity methods, or variational multiscale [39]. The cut-off wave number of the filter is

local within a spectral element. The inclusion of a boundary layer mesh near walls naturally shifts

the effect of the filter to higher wave numbers, eliminating the need for specialized wall treatment.

The LES model in NekRS/Nek5000 has been validated for mixed convection and natural convection

problems [40, 41, 42, 43]. Additionally, the low-Mach approximation can be used to account for

density change [44, 43]. The governing equations solved by NekRS when using the high-pass filter

[38] and the low-Mach number approximation are [45] shown below:

 ‚ąāu
 ŌĀ + u ¬∑ ‚ąáu = ‚ąí‚ąáP + ‚ąáŌĄ + gŌĀ ‚ąíŌĀŌá (u ‚ąíG ‚ąóu) (1)
 ‚ąāt

 ‚ąá¬∑u = ‚ąí1 dŌĀ ‚ąāT + u¬∑‚ąáT (2)

 where ŌĄ = ¬Ķ[‚ąáu+(‚ąáu)T ‚ąí23‚ąá¬∑uI] is the shear stress tensor, G‚ąóis the convolution operator

described above, and Ōá is the filter weight. In the cases studied in this work, we only filtered

the last mode, with a filter weight of 5%, to provide a stable and sufficient solution. Moreover,

the 2nd-order time accuracy is applied in the simulation, in order to achieve a balance between

efficiency and accuracy.

 The implementation of the low-Mach number approximation in NekRS decouples the pressure

from the velocity leading to a system of equations that can be solved numerically in a similar fashion

as the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations. The continuity constraint is redefined by replacing

the material derivative of the temperature with the right hand side of the temperature equation

 ‚ąá¬∑u = ‚ąí1 dŌĀ 1 (‚ąáőļ‚ąáT) (4)
 ŌĀ dT ŌĀCp

 NekRS requires a pure hexahedral mesh to achieve superior computational speed. However,

generating a pure hexahedral mesh for a complex geometry is always challenging. A previous

attempt to create a pure hexahedral mesh for a randomly packed pebble bed leverages the robust

tet-to-hex method [13, 46]. The tet-to-hex method first generates a pure tetrahedral mesh for

a complex geometry and then divides each tetrahedral element into four hexahedral elements.

However, the tet-to-hex approach generates high element counts, generally around 5000 elements

per pebble. This limits its application for a greater number of pebbles. Therefore, the Voronoi

cell approach [12] is applied instead to reduce the element count. Fig. (5) explains the Voronoi

cell approach, showing a Voronoi cell that surrounding a pebble on the left side of figure. The

tessellated surfaces are also shown Fig. (5) right, where quads are extruded towards pebble to

construct hexahedral elements. Using this method, mesh size can be greatly reduced from 5000

elements per pebble to a minimum of 300 elements per pebble.

 The handling of contact points is important in mesh generation. Generally speaking, in

any CFD simulation, a zero area or volume mesh creates a singularity that cannot be handled

numerically. To avoid this, compromise is needed. This is very important for the simulation of

a random pebble bed, as there are pebble-to-pebble contacts throughout the domain. In reality,

 12

Fig. 5. Left: voronoi cell surrounding a pebble, Right: voronoi cell with all quad surfaces

when pebbles contact each other or a wall, the surface area becomes non-zero due to deformation.

However, the deformation is very small due to the large Young‚Äôs Modulus of graphite. From

a numerical perspective, this kind of geometry cannot be meshed. In literature, the common

approach is to increase the pebble diameter to enlarge the contact area, or decrease the pebble-

diameter to avoid contact altogether . However, changing the pebble diameter leads to a porosity

change, which results in a significant change in pressure drop and heat transfer. The alternative

is to add fillets or chamfers to avoid zero-area contact, while maintaining a minimal influence on

porosity.

 For this work, we have chosen to use chamfers to simplify pebble-to-pebble and pebble-to-

wall contacts, to ensure porosity change is negligible. Moreover, the flow is usually stagnate in the

region consumed by chamfers. Therefore, the usage of chamfers can be justified. We can consider

a chamfer a small cylinder that connects two pebbles or a pebble and the wall. Two parameters

are needed to define the chamfers. The first one is the diameter of the connecting cylinder,

Dch. The second parameter is the chamfer detection criteria, Dde. When two pebbles have a

center-to-center distance less than Dde, then a chamfer is added. The same concept is applied to

pebble-to-wall contact. Figure 6 shows the concept of pebble-to-pebble contact with a chamfer,

including the definition of Dch and Dde. For the meshes used in this paper, Dch = 0.1Dpebble,

and Dde = 1.025Dpebble. The values of Dch and Dde are determined based on a compromise

between convergence, resolution, and time-step. A sensitivity study of these two parameters is not

performed here but will be conducted in future work.

 13

Fig. 6. Cross section of two pebbles with a chamfer

 In summary, in this study, we adopted the NekRS code with GPU acceleration. Its strong

scalability allows us to leverage it for large and complex systems. With GPU acceleration, we

achieved significantly better performance compared to previous attempts. However, the approach

presented in this paper still has some limitations. For instance, a relatively large computational

resource is required for such complex systems. Additionally, a more complex meshing strategy is

needed, as a pure hexahedral mesh is required.

IV. EMPIRICAL CORRELATIONS

 Simulations of KPH-size pebble bed were performed in order to provide additional insight

into fluid flow and heat transfer physics. From the simulation data, we can extract parameters

like pressure drop, heat transfer coefficient and Nusselt number (Nu) to compare with empirical

correlations that are implemented in reduced order codes. The Nusselt number in the pebble bed

is defined in Eq. (5).

simulation with several correlations obtained from literature. However, different correlations used

V. VALIDATION AGAINST EXPERIMENTAL DATA

 In this section, we validate our numerical approach against an experiment performed by Lee et

al. [48]. The experiment facility contains 1350 pebbles, allowing us to perform multiple simulations.

The pressure gradient was obtained from NekRS simulations at five different Reynolds numbers,

Re = 324, 503, 681, 857, and 1035. The numerical data was compared with the KTA pressure drop

correlation [49] for a random pebble bed. The comparison of pressure gradient between experiment,

simulation, and correlations is shown in Fig. (7).

 The velocity was measured on a plane of the pebble bed with PIV (Particle Image Velocime-

try). Fig. (8) presents the comparison of velocity on one sampling line at Re = 1460. As we can

see in both Figs. (7 & 8), NekRS can successfully reproduce the flow in a random pebble bed

geometry, thus validating our numerical approach.

VI. VERIFICATION AGAINST OTHER CODE

 The verification of NekRS against other geometries such as channel, pipe, structured pebble

bed can be found in references [40] [41] [43]. Therefore, duplicated discussion is avoided here, and

we focus on random pebble bed only. In this section, we verify the usage of NekRS for random

pebble bed flow against the open-source CFD code OpenFOAM [50]. The comparison is based on

a smaller bed with 1568 pebbles for gas flow, which has been studied extensively in references [17]

and [51]. In reference [52], we also showed a similar comparison for salt flow but in a different

pebble bed. Therefore, we avoid demonstrating the same data in this paper.

VII. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

 In this section, we first discuss heat transfer data, including the Nusselt number (Nu),

Grashof number (Gr), and Richardson number (Ri). Primarily, we compare the numerical data

with different correlations for Nu. Furthermore, the numerical domain is divided into smaller

annular subsections for local data comparison. Then, we discuss the pressure drop data and its

comparison with correlations.

VII.A. Heat Transfer

 In a pebble bed core, the near wall region has a different value of porosity from the center

part. Therefore, we divide the core into the center (R < 11) and outer (11 < R < 14) regions

as shown in Fig. (14). Entrance and exit effects are eliminated by ignoring a length of 2 pebble-

diameters from the beginning and end of the pebble bed. The simulation results are also compared

with the Gnielinski [20], Wakao [21, 22], Whitaker [23], and KTA [24] correlations. The porosity of

the center core region and outer core region are 0.376 and 0.417 respectively. Figure 15 summarizes

the Nusselt number from simulation and correlations for the full core, center, and outer regions

separately for all cases shown in Table I. As we can see, most correlations show a difference of less

than 20%, except the one from KTA. Moreover, the prediction from Wakao‚Äôs correlation matches

simulation data exceptionally well in full and center core regions, but slightly underperforms in

outer core region than Gnielinski‚Äôs.

 20

Fig. 14. The Nusselt number calculation region in Hermes core. the center core region is marked
by light red. The outer core region is marked by light purple. left: y-plane cross-section view,
right: z-plane cross-section view.

 In order to assess the wall effect, we divide the domain into annular subsections for analysis.

The division is done both axially (5 slices) and radially (10 slices) as can be seen in Fig. (16). The

axial slices are evenly distributed with a thickness of 8Dpebble. The radial slices are also evenly

distributed with the exception of the innermost subsection, which is cylindrical with a radius of

R1 = 14Rbed.5 The remaining annular subsections have a radial thickness of 14Rbed(=1 1Dpebble).

 Figure 17 presents the volume-averaged axial velocity distribution, volume-averaged fluid

temperature, and surface-averaged pebble surface temperature in each subsection converted to

dimensional values for Case 1 (Re = 160). In meantime, Fig. (18) and Fig. (19) show the same

data but for Case 2 (Re = 500) and 3 (Re = 1000). In Case 1, velocity data at slice 1 (at the

bottom of the core where flow just enter the bed), the outer core region velocity is higher than the

center core region due to the higher local porosity. This observation is consistent with any forced

flow in a pebble bed. However, for other slices in Case 1, the buoyancy effect and density variation

accelerate the fluid near the core center, resulting in a center-peaked profile. For Case 2 and 3, at

higher Re and lower ‚ąÜT, forced flow dominate the flow and density variation is small. Thus, an

acceleration of the flow is not observed, as axial velocity profiles are similar at different heights.

In Fig. (17), Fig. (18) and Fig. (19), we also present the pebble surface and fluid temperatures

for each case. As observed, in all cases, both the fluid and pebble surface temperatures gradually

 The distributions of Relocal, Gr, Ri ,and Nu are plotted in Fig. (20), Fig. (21) and Fig. (22)

are between 1 and 10, indicating that flow is in the mixed-convection regime. In this regime, both

forced flow and natural convection are important. However for Case 2 and 3 (Fig. (21(c)) and

Fig. (22(c))), Ri is below 1, suggesting that forced-convection dominate the flow and buoyancy

is negligible. The Nu data for Cases 2 and 3 have a similar profile, but are elevated at different

heights. The wall effect is insignificant in Cases 2 and 3, as there is only a small drop in the value

of Nu. However, in Case 1, the wall effect becomes very prominent. This is partially due to the

strong buoyancy and wall cooling conditions.

VII.B. Pressure drop

 The dimensional pressure gradients versus Re in Cases 1, 2, and 3 are shown in Figure 26.

The direct comparison between simulation and correlations is presented in Figure 27. To avoid

entrance and exit form loss, the pressure difference is sampled two pebble-diameters into the pebble

bed. As we can see, all correlations predict a good agreement with the numerical data, considering

their uncertainty. But in general, Reger‚Äôs correlation outperforms the other two.

VIII. CONCLUSIONS

 In this paper, we presented a study of a Hermes-size pebble bed core using the Spectral

Element Method CFD code NekRS. The work flow uses the Voronoi-cell strategy to generate the

mesh, demonstrating the robustness of this approach for a random pebble bed. The speedup

achieved by using a GPU-accelerated CFD code makes a full-size pebble bed core simulation

feasible with currently available computational resources. The Hermes-size case was unprecedented

in terms of geometric complexity, mesh size, and computational speed. It provides high-fidelity

information about the flow and heat transfer in an engineering-scale pebbled bed reactor with

molten salt flow at a level of detail that would be very difficult, if not impossible to achieve

experimentally. As remark, the developed case in this work is the first of its kind in terms of

the size and complexity. As high-fidelity computation becomes achievable on larger scales, more

thorough studies for a wider range of conditions and sensitivity analyses can be performed. In the

meantime, the data present in this study can be valuable as a benchmark for lower fidelity tools,

as well as a reference for confirmation of existing correlations‚Äô applicability.

 In this paper, we first discussed the problem and numerical method. Then we presented

validation and verification against experimental and numerical data respectively based on smaller

beds. Finally, we performed LES fluid-domain-only simulations at various Reynolds numbers (Re)

for the Hermes-size pebble bed core. The global and local Nusselt numbers were obtained for three

cases at different Re. For Case 1, at low Re (160) and high ‚ąÜT, a stronger buoyancy effect is

exhibited. Due to variable density and buoyancy effects, the velocity in the center becomes higher

than in the periphery. The resulting Richardson number (Ri) is between 1 and 10 throughout the

entire domain, indicating that both forced flow and natural convection are significant. For Cases

2 and 3, at higher Re (500, 1000) and lower ‚ąÜT, the flow has a Ri below 1, suggesting that forced

flow dominates. The Nusselt number at different subsections was calculated and compared with

empirical correlations. Generally speaking, three of the four tested correlations give reasonable

predictions of the Nusselt number. The KTA correlation is the notable outlier, over-predicting

the Nusselt number by more than 20 %. The Gnielinski and Whitaker correlations both show

reasonable agreement, typically within 10-20 %. Wakao‚Äôs correlation significantly outperforms

the others in this study. Thus, we recommend using Wakao‚Äôs correlation in the porous media

model for the mixed-convection and forced-convection flow regime. Moreover, we also compared

pressure drop data from simulations with correlations. The DNS-derived correlation of Reger, has

the best agreement with the NekRS data with difference below 5 %. While, the KTA performs

better than Ergun‚Äôs among existing experimental base correlations, NekRS results fall within KTA

uncertainty of 15 %. In conclusion, based on the numerical data obtained in this work, we suggest

to use Wakao‚Äôs and Reger‚Äôs correlations for heat transfer and pressure drop respectively.

 In future work, we propose continued investigations using high-fidelity methods such as LES

to better understand the physical mechanisms governing near-wall heat transfer. Additionally, we

 32

suggest including the solid domain and thermal radiation in future work. The inclusion of the

solid domain can study the temperature profile inside solid pebbles, which is important for safety

analysis. Moreover, thermal radiation can become significant in cases of mixed convection.

FOREWORD

It is generally recognized that long term development of nuclear power as part of the world's future
energy mix will require fast reactor technology with a closed fuel cycle. Fast reactors in the closed
fuel cycle represent both the cornerstone of and a bridge to more sustainable nuclear energy
production. In the past few decades, fast reactors have been brought to a high level of maturity by
the design, construction and operation of experimental and prototype reactors. A number of
countries are actively developing fast reactors and there are several demonstration projects, of a
variety of sizes, under study or under construction. Progress has been made as well in the
development of related fuel cycles with processes being demonstrated at pilot plant scale.

The IAEA has been supporting the development and deployment of fast reactor technology and
serving interested Member States for almost five decades. The Technical Working Group on Fast
Reactors (TWG-FR) and the Technical Working Group on Nuclear Fuel Cycle Options and Spent
Fuel Management (TWG-NFCO) comprise groups of experts providing advice and support for the
implementation of IAEA programmatic activities, reflecting a global network of excellence and
expertise in the areas of advanced technologies and R&D. Among the wide range of activities and
initiatives carried out under the aegis of these two working groups, the International Conference on
Fast Reactors and Related Fuel Cycles is a major event.

The first International Conference on Fast Reactors and Related Fuel Cycles: Challenges and
Opportunities (FR09) was held in Kyoto, hosted by the Government of Japan. The event provided
an appropriate forum to achieve the main objectives of sharing knowledge and exchanging
information, experience and innovative ideas among the more than 500 experts from 20 countries
and 3 international organizations in attendance. The IAEA organized the second conference, on the
theme of Safe Technologies and Sustainable Scenarios (FR13), in Paris, in 2013, hosted by the
Government of France. This second event was attended by almost 600 experts from 27 countries
and 4 international organizations representing different fields of fast reactor and related fuel cycle
technologies. Continuing this effort, in 2017 the IAEA organized the third international conference,
on the theme of Next Generation Nuclear Systems for Sustainable Development (FR17). Held in
Yekaterinburg and hosted by the Government of the Russian Federation, the FR17 conference was
attended by some550 participants from 27 countries and 6 international organizations.

The purpose of the FR17 conference was to provide a forum to exchange information on national
and international programmes, and more generally on new developments and experience, in the
field of fast reactors and related fuel cycle technologies. By providing a scientific platform for
experienced scientists, engineers, government officials, safety officers and fast reactor managers to
share their perspectives, the FR17 conference also facilitated the exchange of knowledge between
generations. Such exchange can help in choosing the correct path of research to meet the upcoming
challenges in the development of fast reactors and related fuel cycles.

As an output of the successful organization of the FR17 conference, these Proceedings provide a
summary of the different technical, plenary and young generation event sessions, as well as the full
text of the opening, closing and plenary speeches delivered during the conference.

The IAEA would like to express its appreciation to the Government of the Russian Federation for
hosting the conference through the State Atomic Energy Corporation ‚ÄúRosatom‚ÄĚ, and to the
members of the International Advisory Committee, the International Scientific Programme
Committee, the Local Organizational Committee and the Secretariat of the Conference for the
commitment shown in organizing and convening the FR17 conference.

The IAEA officers responsible for this publication were V. Kriventsev of the Division of Nuclear
Power and A. Gonz√°lez-Espartero of the Division of Nuclear Fuel Cycle and Waste Technology.
